one_line_takeaway: TCRL adds a worst-case-cost Bellman constraint plus a dual reward-autocorrelation/entropy-stability defense to PPO-Lagrange CRL, cutting safety-cost violations by up to ~190x and raising reward up to ~34.76% versus prior robust-CRL baselines under a newly formalized escalating ('temporal-coupled') adversarial perturbation attack across four Bullet-Safety-Gym robot-control tasks.
key_finding: TCRL-PPOL achieves the lowest safety cost of all methods under the Worst-TC attacker in every one of the four tasks (e.g. Car-Run: cost 0.18+/-0.26 for TCRL-PPOL vs 30.78, 34.52, 0.36 for PPOL-vanilla/PPOL-random/ADV-PPOL(MC) respectively; Ball-Circle: 3.84+/-6.45 vs 75.44, 72.80, 25.32). The paper reports that, relative to the three baselines, TCRL reduces safety cost under Worst-TC by 559.38%-1864.58% on Ball-Circle, 1779.49%-15,383.33% on Ball-Run, 14.29%-1514.29% on Car-Circle, and 100.00%-19,077.78% on Car-Run (matching the abstract's claim of up to 190.77x cost reduction). TCRL also achieves higher reward under Worst-TC than the baselines (11.09%-26.47% higher on Ball-Circle, 8.36%-16.73% on Ball-Run, 14.62%-34.76% on Car-Circle, 24.04%-33.58% on Car-Run), and notably TCRL's reward under Worst-TC attack (e.g. Ball-Run: 1154.93) exceeds its own natural (unattacked) reward (1031.59), which the authors attribute to its defense-oriented, lower-cost natural-condition policy becoming advantageous when attacked. Against the MC attacker specifically, ADV-PPOL(MC) -- which is directly trained against that same attacker -- retains the lowest cost, since TCRL was not specifically tuned to that attacker family. Ablations show removing the worst-case cost constraint (TCRL-Worstcost) increases safety cost by 13.70 (Ball-Circle), 26.04 (Ball-Run), 2.37 (Car-Circle), 20.34 (Car-Run) under Worst-TC; removing the reward-based dual defense (TCRL-reward) reduces reward by 121.12, 89.38, 51.46, 154.96 respectively on the same four tasks -- demonstrating each component contributes a distinct, complementary effect (cost containment vs reward preservation).
core_contribution: The paper proposes TCRL (Temporal-Coupled adversarial training for Robust constrained RL), a training framework for constrained reinforcement learning (CRL) that defends against a newly formalized class of 'temporal-coupled' adversarial state-observation perturbations, where the attacker's per-step perturbation budget grows over time based on the magnitude of its previous perturbation (Eq. 2-3), rather than being a fixed-radius i.i.d. per-step attack as in prior robust-CRL work. TCRL has two components: (1) a worst-case-perceiving cost constraint function, built on a novel 'worst-case cost Bellman operator' (Definition 3, Theorem 4.1) that estimates the worst-case safety cost achievable under bounded state perturbations without explicitly modeling/training an adversary policy, used to replace the standard expected-cost CRL constraint with a worst-case-cost constraint (Eq. 8); and (2) a dual reward-based defense: a temporal autocorrelation penalty on the reward sequence (Eq. 9-11) to prevent an attacker from modeling reward dynamics over time, and an entropy-stability constraint on the reward distribution (Eq. 12-14) to bound how fast reward unpredictability can change. Both constraints are combined with the RL objective via Lagrangian relaxation (Eq. 15-16) and instantiated on top of the PID-PPO-Lagrange (PPOL) algorithm to produce TCRL-PPOL.
threat_model: Test-time (deployment-time) evasion/perturbation attack on a trained CRL policy's state observations, not a training-time poisoning or LLM-facing attack. The attacker perturbs the true state s_t into a corrupted observation within an l_p-norm ball of radius epsilon (bounded perturbation, white/gray-box access to the policy's value/cost critics, per Eq. 17 which uses Q_r and Q_c). The paper's novel threat is that this per-step budget is temporally coupled: the allowed perturbation at step t is anchored to the perturbation magnitude at step t-1 via an adaptive radius epsilon_bar_t = epsilon_bar_{t-1} + alpha*||p_{t-1}|| (Definition 1), letting the attacker escalate perturbation intensity over a trajectory while keeping perturbations smooth/stable, as opposed to prior robust-CRL threat models which assume independent, fixed-radius perturbations at each step. The paper's own worst-case attacker (Worst-TC) is constructed by jointly using a worst-case cost estimator and a reward-minimization estimator (Eq. 17) to select the perturbed action that most degrades reward while maximizing cost.
limitations: (1) The worst-case cost network relies on convex-relaxation techniques (referenced from prior IBP-style verified-training work) to bound Omega(s,pi), which the paper's related-work section itself notes elsewhere (of other verification-based methods) can suffer combinatorial explosion or scaling difficulty in continuous action spaces -- it is not explicitly re-verified for TCRL's own network scale. (2) Evaluation is restricted to four Bullet-Safety-Gym Robot-Task combinations (Ball/Car x Circle/Run), all low-dimensional continuous-control simulation tasks, not real hardware or higher-dimensional/vision-based CRL domains. (3) The specialized-attacker asymmetry: TCRL is not the best performer against the MC attacker (ADV-PPOL(MC), trained specifically against MC, retains lowest cost there), indicating TCRL's temporal-coupled defense does not dominate every attacker type uniformly. (4) High variance is visible in several natural/attacked reward and cost entries in Table 1 (e.g. std sometimes exceeding the mean, as in PPOL-vanilla Ball-Circle MAD reward 728.05+/-1483.51), which is not discussed as a statistical caveat in the excerpted text. (5) The theoretical convergence claim (iterates converge almost surely to a local minimum, Section 4.5) is stated but its proof is deferred entirely to the Appendix, which was not in the extracted page range.

datasets_used: Bullet-Safety-Gym benchmark (Gronauer 2022): Ball-Circle, Ball-Run, Car-Circle, Car-Run Robot-Task simulation environments
quantitative_results: Safety cost reduction under Worst-TC attacker vs baselines: 559.38%-1864.58% (Ball-Circle), 1779.49%-15,383.33% (Ball-Run), 14.29%-1514.29% (Car-Circle), 100.00%-19,077.78% (Car-Run)
Reward improvement under Worst-TC attacker vs baselines: 11.09%-26.47% (Ball-Circle), 8.36%-16.73% (Ball-Run), 14.62%-34.76% (Car-Circle), 24.04%-33.58% (Car-Run)
Abstract-level summary: up to 190.77x reduction in safety cost and up to 1.34x increase in reward

Table 1 Worst-TC cost, TCRL-PPOL vs PPOL-vanilla/PPOL-random/ADV-PPOL(MC): Ball-Circle 3.84+/-6.45 vs 75.44/72.80/25.32; Ball-Run 1.56+/-1.41 vs 241.54/140.37/29.32; Car-Circle 2.38+/-1.53 vs 2.72/38.42/2.80; Car-Run 0.18+/-0.26 vs 30.78/34.52/0.36
Ablation (Table 2): removing worst-case cost constraint (TCRL-Worstcost) increases Worst-TC cost by 13.70 (Ball-Circle), 26.04 (Ball-Run), 2.37 (Car-Circle), 20.34 (Car-Run)
Ablation (Table 2): removing reward-based dual defense (TCRL-reward) reduces Worst-TC reward by 121.12 (Ball-Circle), 89.38 (Ball-Run), 51.46 (Car-Circle), 154.96 (Car-Run)
baselines_compared: ADV-PPOL(MC) (adversarially trained against a Max-Cost attacker)
